**Ruggero Grieco** (19 August 1893 – 23 July 1955) was an Italian politician, antifascist, and member of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (*Italian: Partito Comunista Italiano, PCI*).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruggero_Grieco&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Born in [Foggia](/wiki/Foggia "Foggia"), [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), Grieco completed high school studies at the institute of agronomy. Aged 18 he came into contact with socialist circles and met [Amadeo Bordiga](/wiki/Amadeo_Bordiga "Amadeo Bordiga"). In 1912 he joined the Socialist Party. After Italy's entry in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in 1915, Grieco served as a second lieutenant in the army.

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruggero_Grieco&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political career")]

In 1921 he took part in the founding of the Italian Communist Party. From 1921 he was a member of the party's central committee and executive committee, siding with Bordiga during the [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno") schism. In 1927 he became a member first of the party's politburo and then of its leadership. From 1924 to 1926 he was a parliamentary deputy.

## Exile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruggero_Grieco&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Exile")]

Between 1926 and 1944 he was in exile in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). He became a member of the Foreign Center of the Italian Communist Party under the pseudonym "Garlandi." In 1928 he became a candidate member and in 1935 a member of the Executive Committee of the Communist International. During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") he worked on radio system in Moscow, where he was in charge of broadcasts for Italy.

## Return to Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruggero_Grieco&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Return to Italy")]

In 1944 he returned to Italy and took over the propaganda section of the Communist Party and headed the agrarian committee of the Party's central committee. He was editor of the magazine *La riforma agraria* (Agrarian Reform). In 1946 he was elected to the Constituent Assembly and in 1948 became a senator.

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruggero_Grieco&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death")]

He died of a heart attack at [Massa Lombarda](/wiki/Massa_Lombarda "Massa Lombarda"), during a political meeting to mark the launch of the National Farmworkers' Alliance (*Alleanza nazionale dei contadini*)

## Further reading (in Italian)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ruggero_Grieco&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Further reading (in Italian)")]

* [Giorgio Amendola](/wiki/Giorgio_Amendola "Giorgio Amendola"). *Storia del Partito Comunista Italiano 1921-1943*, Roma, [Editori Riuniti](/wiki/Editori_Riuniti "Editori Riuniti"), 1966.
* Giorgio Amendola, *Scritti scelti*. Roma, Editori Riuniti, 1966.
* Paolo Spriano, *Storia del Partito Comunista Italiano*, Torino, Einaudi, 1967-1975
* Michele Pistillo, *Il pensiero e l'opera di Ruggero Grieco: Le Tesi agrarie di Lione*, in «Rinascita», 46, 1983.
* Michele Pistillo. *Vita di Ruggero Grieco*. Roma, Editori Riuniti, 1985.
* Bruno Grieco, *Un partito non stalinista, Pci 1936: «Appello ai fratelli in camicia nera»*, Venezia, Marsilio, 2004.
